---
title: "Data and the Average"
subtitle: "Many numbers, one summary"
lesson-id: "t1.data-and-average"
tier: 1
prerequisites: ["t1.divide-fair-share"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "databases", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](../06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)?
2. Can you compute $84 \div 7 = 12$?
3. Have you ever heard a sports commentator say "his batting average is
   45" or seen "average rainfall: 800 mm"? That number is the topic of
   this lesson.
:::

## Touch

Adjust the dataset (5 numbers). Watch the **mean** (average), **median**
(middle), and **range** (spread) update.

```{ojs}
viewof a = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 20, step: 1, label: "Value 1:"})
viewof b = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 30, step: 1, label: "Value 2:"})
viewof c = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 40, step: 1, label: "Value 3:"})
viewof d = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 50, step: 1, label: "Value 4:"})
viewof e = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 60, step: 1, label: "Value 5:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const data = [a, b, c, d, e];
  const sorted = [...data].sort((p, q) => p - q);
  const sum = data.reduce((s, v) => s + v, 0);
  const mean = sum / data.length;
  const median = sorted[2];
  const range = sorted[4] - sorted[0];

  const W = 720, H = 220, pad = 40;
  const xMin = 0, xMax = 100;
  const x = v => pad + (v / xMax) * (W - 2 * pad);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px;");

  // axis
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", pad).attr("x2", W - pad)
    .attr("y1", H - 50).attr("y2", H - 50)
    .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  for (let v = 0; v <= 100; v += 10) {
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", x(v)).attr("x2", x(v))
      .attr("y1", H - 56).attr("y2", H - 44).attr("stroke", "#1f2933");
    svg.append("text").attr("x", x(v)).attr("y", H - 30)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "11").text(v);
  }
  // data points
  data.forEach((v, i) => {
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", x(v)).attr("cy", H - 80)
      .attr("r", 7).attr("fill", "#90caf9").attr("stroke", "#2c5f7c");
    svg.append("text").attr("x", x(v)).attr("y", H - 90)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "10").attr("fill", "#666")
      .text(v);
  });
  // mean marker (vertical line)
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", x(mean)).attr("x2", x(mean))
    .attr("y1", H - 110).attr("y2", H - 50)
    .attr("stroke", "#c2185b").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", x(mean)).attr("y", H - 115)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "12").attr("fill", "#c2185b").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(`mean = ${mean.toFixed(1)}`);
  // median marker (vertical line)
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", x(median)).attr("x2", x(median))
    .attr("y1", H - 110).attr("y2", H - 50)
    .attr("stroke", "#2e7d32").attr("stroke-width", 2).attr("stroke-dasharray", "4 3");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", x(median)).attr("y", H - 130)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "12").attr("fill", "#2e7d32").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(`median = ${median}`);
  // range
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad).attr("y", 28)
    .attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#666")
    .text(`Range (max − min): ${sorted[4]} − ${sorted[0]} = ${range}`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

The **mean** (average) and the **median** (middle value) usually agree —
but not always. Try setting one value to 100 and the others to 5.
Notice how the *mean* shifts much more than the *median*.

## Wonder

1. Set all five values to the same number — say 40. What is the mean?
   The median?
2. Set four values to 10 and one to 100. What is the mean? The median?
   Which is more "honest" about the typical value?
3. If you knew only the mean of a dataset, could you reconstruct the
   original numbers?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Three friends took a test. Their scores were **80, 75, 85** out of 100.
Find the **mean** (average) score.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Mean = sum of values ÷ count.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum: $80 + 75 + 85 = 240$. Count: 3. Mean: $240 \div 3 = 80$. **80.**

The mean is "if everyone had the same score, what would it be so that
the total stays the same?" — it spreads the total evenly.
</details>

### Problem 2

Ten children's heights (in cm): **120, 125, 130, 132, 132, 135, 138,
140, 142, 150**. Find the **median** (middle value).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For an *even* number of values, the median is the average of the two
middle ones.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Already sorted. Ten values → middle two are the 5th and 6th: **132** and
**135**. Median: $(132 + 135) \div 2 = 133.5$ cm.

For an *odd* count, the median is just the single middle value (no
averaging needed). The median is robust against extreme values — it
doesn't get pulled by outliers the way the mean does.
</details>

### Problem 3

Five workers earn (in thousand rupees per month): **30, 32, 35, 38, 175**
(the last is the boss). Compute the **mean** and the **median**. Which
is a more honest summary of "what a typical worker earns"?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Mean: sum ÷ count. Median: middle of the sorted list.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum: $30 + 32 + 35 + 38 + 175 = 310$. Mean: $310 \div 5 = 62$.
Median (middle of sorted [30,32,35,38,175]): **35**.

The mean is **62 thousand**, but four of the five workers earn well
below that. The single outlier (the boss at 175) drags the mean up. The
median (**35 thousand**) reflects the typical worker more honestly.

This is one of the most important practical points about averages:
**when data is skewed (one extreme value), the median tells the truth
better than the mean**. News stories about "average income" almost always
mean the *median* income for this exact reason.
</details>

### Problem 4

The mean of five numbers is **20**. What is their sum?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Mean = sum ÷ count, so sum = mean × count.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum $= 20 \times 5 = 100$. Multiplication and division are inverses
(Lesson 6); algebra (Lesson 11) gives the same answer in symbols:
$\bar{x} = S/n$ implies $S = \bar{x} \cdot n$.

Knowing the mean and the count is *equivalent* to knowing the sum, but
*not* equivalent to knowing the individual values. The mean is a
*compression* of the data — it loses information.
</details>

### Problem 5

A class of 5 students has test scores **40, 60, 70, 80, 90**. What is
the **range**? What does it tell you that the mean and median don't?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Range = largest − smallest.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Range $= 90 - 40 = 50$.

The range tells you about the **spread** of the data — how far apart
the values are. Two classes can have the *same mean* (both 68) but very
different *ranges*: one might be tightly clustered (60, 65, 68, 71, 76)
and one widely spread (40, 50, 68, 86, 96). The mean alone can't
distinguish them.

We need at least *two* numbers to summarise a dataset honestly: a
**centre** (mean or median) and a **spread** (range, or more refined
measures we'll meet in Tier 5: variance and standard deviation).
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

Given a list of numbers $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ (**the data**):

- **Sum:** $S = x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n$.
- **Count:** $n$.
- **Mean (average):** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{S}{n}$. The "balance point" of
  the data.
- **Median:** the middle value when the data is sorted (for even $n$,
  the average of the two middle values). The "halfway" value.
- **Range:** largest minus smallest. A measure of *spread*.
- **Mode:** the most frequently occurring value. Useful for
  *categorical* data.

A single summary number throws away information. Honest data reporting
usually requires *both* a centre and a spread.

:::

> *Sum the values, divide by count — that is the mean;*
> *Sort the values, take the middle — that is the median;*
> *Largest minus smallest — that is the spread;*
> *No single number can tell the whole story.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A cricket player's last 6 innings scored: **42, 0, 78, 113, 5, 22**.
> Compute the mean, median, range. Which best describes "his form"?

A competent solver works in steps:

1. **Sum:** $42 + 0 + 78 + 113 + 5 + 22 = 260$.
2. **Mean:** $260 \div 6 \approx 43.3$.
3. **Sorted:** 0, 5, 22, 42, 78, 113. Middle two: 22 and 42. **Median:**
   $(22 + 42)/2 = 32$.
4. **Range:** $113 - 0 = 113$.

**The range tells the real story.** The player is wildly inconsistent —
one century, two single-digit failures, a duck. Saying "his average is
43" is technically true but misleading. *Both* the median (32) and the
huge range (113) reveal the inconsistency. A coach would care about all
three numbers.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Find the mean of: 5, 8, 10, 7, 10.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum 40, count 5, mean **8**.
</details>

**W2.** Find the median of: 12, 7, 15, 9, 20.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sorted: 7, 9, 12, 15, 20. Middle: **12**.
</details>

**W3.** Find the range of: 3, 11, 7, 22, 8, 15.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Max 22, min 3, range **19**.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Six students' marks: **65, 72, 80, 80, 91, 88**. Compute mean,
median, mode.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Mode is the most frequent. Median (even count) is average of middle two.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum: $65 + 72 + 80 + 80 + 91 + 88 = 476$. Mean: $476 \div 6 \approx 79.3$.
Sorted: 65, 72, 80, 80, 88, 91. Middle two: 80 and 80. Median: **80**.
Mode: **80** (appears twice).

All three centre measures are close ($\approx 80$) — a good sign the
data is well-clustered.
</details>

**P2.** A test had a mean score of **62**. There were 25 students. The
sum of all scores is...?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sum = mean × count.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$62 \times 25 = 1{,}550$. **The sum is 1550 marks.**
</details>

**P3.** A box of 10 chocolates has masses (grams): **18, 20, 21, 19, 20,
22, 21, 19, 20, 20**. Compute the mean. Then say which chocolate is
*above* and which is *below* the mean.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum: 200. Mean: **20 g**. Above: the two 21 g and the 22 g. Below: the
18 g and the two 19 g. The four 20 g are exactly *at* the mean.

A useful test: the **deviations** from the mean (each value minus the
mean) sum to *zero* — by construction. Try it: $(-2) + 0 + 1 + (-1) + 0
+ 2 + 1 + (-1) + 0 + 0 = 0$ ✓. This will be central in Tier 5 when we
define **variance**.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A neural network is trained on **batches of data**. For each
batch, it computes the **average loss** and updates its weights based
on the *gradient* of that average. Why average instead of summing?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Imagine batch size 100 vs batch size 10. If we used the sum, would the
loss values be comparable?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Averaging makes losses comparable across batch sizes.** A sum of 100
losses is naturally 10× larger than a sum of 10 losses, even if the
*per-example* loss is identical. By averaging, the loss number depends
only on the *typical* per-example error, not on how many examples
happened to be in the batch.

This matters because the learning rate (Lesson 9) is calibrated for the
*per-example* gradient. Using a sum would force the learning rate to be
adjusted whenever batch size changes — a fragile setup. Averaging
decouples the two.

Almost every loss function in modern ML is an average for this reason.
The humble Tier 1 idea of "mean" is doing real work in cutting-edge
research.
</details>

**S2.** The **median income** in a country is reported as Rs. 25,000 per
month, but the **mean income** is Rs. 80,000 per month. What does this
tell you about the income distribution? Which number would a politician
use, and why?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The mean is much higher than the median, which means a **small number
of very high earners are pulling the mean up** while half the
population earns ≤ 25,000. The income distribution is *right-skewed*
— a long tail of high earners.

A politician arguing the country is doing well might quote the **mean**
(Rs. 80,000 — sounds prosperous). A politician arguing the country needs
intervention might quote the **median** (Rs. 25,000 — reveals the
typical struggle). Both numbers are *true*; both are honest answers to
slightly different questions.

This is why statistically literate citizens demand to know *which*
average is being quoted. "The average X" is meaningless without the
qualifier.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Average always means the mean."** No. "Average" can mean mean,
   median, or mode in ordinary speech. In serious data work, *say which*.
2. **"The mean is the typical value."** Often, but not always. When
   data is skewed (S2), the median is more typical. The mean is the
   *balance point*, not necessarily the typical case.
3. **"You can summarise data with one number."** No — you need at least
   a centre *and* a spread. A mean of 50 with range 1 vs. range 99 are
   wildly different datasets.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain to a friend why the median is more honest than the
   mean for income data?
2. Without scrolling back, can you compute the mean and median of: 4, 7,
   9, 12, 18?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: mean = 50/5 = 10; median = 9.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](../15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** — the income/distribution intuition is crucial
  data literacy.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](../06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)** —
  the mean is total ÷ count, exactly the fair-share calculation.

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 3 — Probability and basic statistics** — frequency tables,
  histograms, expected value.
- **Tier 5 — Probability theory and statistics** — variance, standard
  deviation, sampling, hypothesis testing, regression.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Almost every loss function is an average
(Stretch S1). Many activation functions and metrics involve means
(softmax probabilities, BLEU scores, F1 scores). Data normalisation
subtracts the mean of each feature. Statistical thinking is half of
ML practice.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** SQL's `AVG()`, `MEDIAN()` (in some dialects),
percentile functions, `COUNT()`, `SUM()` are the daily tools of
analytical query writing. Every dashboard, every business report, runs
on aggregations.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** The journey from "mean of a finite list" to
"expected value of a random variable" to "integral with respect to a
probability measure" is one of the great unifications in mathematics —
the subject of measure theory in Tier 7.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Sports statistics, nutrition (per 100 g),
economic indicators (CPI, GDP per capita), grades and rankings,
weather (average temperature), polling. Almost every numerical claim
in public discourse is a kind of average.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the data-points + mean-line
  widget makes "balance point" visual), Boole (the *which is more
  honest?* question is the heart of statistical literacy), Russian
  school (the salary problem in P3 surfaces skew without naming it),
  Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse).
- **Self-study guarantees** all present.
:::
